Or conversely: You say everything is fine because you don’t want to burden anyone. But inside, you’re carrying the whole burden alone. Many people with chronic illnesses are familiar with moments like these. And it’s important to talk about them.

questions.prompt: Why does writing down your needs help?

answers.text: Because it helps you clearly identify what’s important to you and gain clarity about your own desires and boundaries.
answers.explanation: You may sense many things, but it’s only by writing them down that they become clear and tangible. Additionally, the list can be very helpful when communicating with family and friends.

answers.text: Because it allows you to solve your problems immediately.
answers.explanation: Writing things down helps with clarity, but it doesn’t automatically solve all problems.

answers.text: Because it means you have to think less.
answers.explanation: On the contrary—it encourages you to consciously reflect on your own needs.

answers.text: Because it helps you better explain what others need.
answers.explanation: That sounds reasonable, but the main point here is to understand your own needs, not those of others.

questions.prompt: What is the assignment for today’s session?

answers.text: Write two lists: one with things you want to do yourself, and one with areas where you’d like support.
answers.explanation: Right. Writing things down helps you recognize your own needs and boundaries more clearly—because only once you’re clear about them can you communicate them to others.

answers.text: Write a list of everything you haven’t been able to do yourself since your diagnosis.
answers.explanation: It’s not about listing losses, but about what’s important to you—both what you want to keep in your own hands and where you’d like help.

answers.text: Just think about what you need without writing it down.
answers.explanation: Writing things down is an important part of the exercise. While you may sense many things, it is only by putting them into concrete words that true clarity about your own needs emerges.

answers.text: Write down three things for which you would like support.
answers.explanation: This is only part of the task. The goal is to create two lists: one with things you want to handle yourself, and one with areas where you would like support.

detailBodyContains: Perhaps you have asked yourself this question before. And perhaps your answer before the diagnosis was different from what it is today. Many people with a mental health condition report that this answer shifts over time. And suddenly, the answer to “Who am I?” revolves almost entirely around the diagnosis. In this video, you’ll learn: What self-concept is and why it can shift following a diagnosis. What modern psychology has to say about it. And what concrete steps you can take to positively strengthen your self-image.

questions.prompt: What best describes “self-concept”?

answers.text: The image we have of ourselves—that is, what we think, feel, and believe about ourselves.
answers.explanation: Explanation: Self-concept includes, for example, what kind of person you are, what you are capable of, and what is important to you.

answers.text: The physical abilities you have in everyday life.
answers.explanation: Explanation: These can be part of your self-image, but they do not constitute your entire self-concept.

answers.text: The diagnosis and symptoms of an illness.
answers.explanation: Explanation: The illness is only one part of life, not the entire self-image.

answers.text: Other people’s opinions about who you are.
answers.explanation: Explanation: Other people’s opinions can influence your self-image, but self-concept arises primarily from your own beliefs.

questions.prompt: Your self-concept can change due to an illness. What is an approach from Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) that can help with this?

answers.text: You are more than your symptoms and limitations. You are the person who has experienced all of this and who can decide how to deal with it.
answers.explanation: Explanation: ACT emphasizes that the illness is only a part—not the whole person.

answers.text: You should try to ignore the symptoms as much as possible.
answers.explanation: Explanation: ACT is not about ignoring symptoms, but about accepting them while recognizing that you are more than your symptoms.

answers.text: You should focus on getting back to the way you used to be.
answers.explanation: Explanation: This can lead to frustration because it does not take current reality into account.

answers.text: You should learn to focus on the positive despite the symptoms.
answers.explanation: Explanation: That sounds helpful, but ACT isn’t just about positive thinking; it’s about accepting your own situation and seeing yourself as more than just your thoughts and symptoms.

questions.prompt: What does the “new starting point” mean in everyday life?

answers.text: Use your current capacity as a benchmark and plan from there.
answers.explanation: Explanation: Correct. If you can go for a 30-minute walk today, that’s the new 100 percent. From this point, you plan, act, and shape your daily life—not based on past standards.

answers.text: Setting goals that are just as high as they used to be.
answers.explanation: Explanation: Anyone who measures themselves against what was possible in the past will almost inevitably fall short—because the standard hasn’t adapted to current reality. This creates the feeling of no longer being enough.

answers.text: Focusing only on what you can no longer do.
answers.explanation: Explanation: The new starting point doesn’t look at what has been lost, but at what is possible today—and makes exactly that the foundation for daily life.

answers.text: Gradually working your way back toward past achievements.
answers.explanation: Explanation: That sounds sensible, but it maintains the old standard. This approach flips that on its head: The standard isn’t the past—but what is realistically possible today.

questions.prompt: Which question helps actively strengthen one’s self-image?

answers.text: “What kind of person am I—regardless of what I’m currently capable of?”
answers.explanation: Explanation: This question consciously directs attention toward one’s own qualities and values—and away from what the illness has changed. Character and personality remain intact, even if abilities shift.

answers.text: “What could I do better in the past than I can today?”
answers.explanation: Explanation: This question reinforces the comparison with your former self and the feeling of no longer being enough—instead of focusing on what is still there today.

answers.text: “How can I continue to handle as many tasks as possible just as I did before?”
answers.explanation: Explanation: This question maintains the old standard and can lead to feeling overwhelmed, rather than creating a realistic starting point.

answers.text: “Which tasks can I still handle well right now?”
answers.explanation: Explanation: This is a helpful way to look at one’s own capabilities, but it doesn’t go as deep as the question of one’s character and personal values—which exist independently of one’s performance.

questions.prompt: What happens when you measure yourself daily against what you used to be able to do?

answers.text: You almost inevitably fall short because the standard hasn’t adapted to current reality.
answers.explanation: Explanation: If you cook, but less elaborately than before, or go for walks, but not as far—this comparison creates the feeling that you’re no longer enough. Not because you’re worth less, but because the standard is set incorrectly.

answers.text: You motivate yourself to get back to your former level of performance.
answers.explanation: Explanation: This comparison usually creates pressure instead of motivation because it ignores current reality and maintains an unrealistic standard.

answers.text: You gain a better sense of how much you still accomplish despite your illness.
answers.explanation: Explanation: Comparing yourself to the past usually highlights what’s missing, not what’s possible. It’s more helpful to focus on your new starting point.

answers.text: You maintain a realistic view of yourself.
answers.explanation: Explanation: A realistic self-image isn’t formed by comparing yourself to the past, but by focusing on your current capabilities, values, and qualities.

In this video, you’ll learn: What self-concept is and why it can shift following a diagnosis. What modern psychology has to say about it. And what concrete steps you can take to positively strengthen your self-image.

questions.prompt: Immediately dismissing a compliment is a sign of the “rejecting the positive” mindset. Choose a helpful alternative for the following example: Your friends compliment your new haircut. You think: “Oh, they’re just saying that to make me feel better.”

answers.text: “My friends think my haircut looks nice, and I’m allowed to accept that.”
answers.explanation: This thought allows the positive in and accepts the compliment.

answers.text: “That’s nice of them, but it’s really nothing special.”
answers.explanation: It sounds modest, but it still devalues the positive.

answers.text: “My hair used to look better than it does now.”
answers.explanation: Comparing it to the past distracts from the positive moment.

answers.text: “They just want to cheer me up, so I’m not taking it seriously.”
answers.explanation: That is exactly the rejection of the positive.

questions.prompt: When a single moment is taken as proof of who you are as a person, it’s called “generalization to the self.” Choose a helpful alternative to the following thought: “I didn’t manage to go for my walk today. I’m no longer the kind of person who exercises regularly.”

answers.text: “Exercising was hard for me today. That doesn’t mean I’m someone who can’t exercise anymore.”
answers.explanation: This thought separates a single moment from your own identity.

answers.text: “I should have tried harder today.”
answers.explanation: This thought creates pressure and isn’t helpful.

answers.text: “Maybe I should just stop going for walks altogether if it’s not working out anymore.”
answers.explanation: That reinforces withdrawal and generalization.

answers.text: “Today was difficult; I can’t do what I used to be able to do anymore.”
answers.explanation: That’s more helpful, but it doesn’t address the false generalization about oneself as directly.

In this video, you’ll learn: What self-concept is and why it can shift following a diagnosis. What modern psychology has to say about it. And what concrete steps you can take to positively strengthen your self-image.

questions.prompt: Why is it sometimes so hard to recognize our own strengths?

answers.text: Because the brain is trained to notice mistakes and weaknesses more readily.
answers.explanation: Explanation: Our brain often automatically focuses on the negative—we need practice and repetition to learn to notice the positive more consciously.

answers.text: Because we don’t have enough time in our daily lives to think about it.
answers.explanation: Explanation: Time can play a role, but it’s not the main reason.

answers.text: Because you don’t need to improve your strengths, but should only work on your weaknesses.
answers.explanation: Explanation: Both are important—the problem is more a matter of one-sided perception.

answers.text: Because you can only recognize your strengths when everything is going well.
answers.explanation: Explanation: That sounds reasonable, but strengths also show up in small things—not just on good days.

questions.prompt: What is your task for the next seven days?

answers.text: Write down every day what you did well that day—one sentence is enough.
answers.explanation: Explanation: Correct. The exercise consciously focuses attention on what is there—not on what was possible in the past or what someone else might have done. Writing it down helps you notice strengths that the brain would otherwise easily overlook.

answers.text: Write down three things every day that you’re grateful for.
answers.explanation: Explanation: Gratitude is a different approach. This exercise asks a very specific question: “What did I do well today?”—the focus is on your own strengths and achievements of the day.

answers.text: At the end of the week, look back and write down what went well over the past seven days.
answers.explanation: Explanation: This exercise is intended as a daily routine—not as a weekly review. This is the only way to train yourself to regularly notice the positive aspects of the day.

answers.text: Write down what you’d like to do better tomorrow.
answers.explanation: Explanation: This exercise doesn’t look ahead, but consciously focuses on today. The question is: “What did I do well today?”—not what should be better tomorrow.
